messages.content: in ogre3d 1.7, if you apply a 1 degree rotation on all its axis in a single node at any initial orientation with an attached entity. what behavior does it do?

In Ogre3D 1.7, if you apply a 1 degree rotation on all its axes in a single node at any initial orientation with an attached entity, the behavior would be a continuous rotation of the entity around all three axes. It means that the entity will rotate slightly (1 degree) on each axis with each frame update.

This behavior will result in a spinning motion of the entity in 3D space. The speed of the rotation will depend on the frame rate and can be adjusted by altering the rotation value or the frame rate itself.
ok but if i have a node that has separate nodes for its xyz, it does not roll the same and it wobbles. right?

Yes, if you have separate nodes for each axis of rotation (X, Y, Z), and apply a 1-degree rotation on each axis individually, the behavior will be different compared to applying the rotation on a single node.

When applying the rotation individually on separate nodes for each axis, it will result in a movement known as "gimbal lock" or "wobbling". Gimbal lock occurs when two of the rotational axes become aligned, causing a loss of one degree of freedom. This can lead to unexpected or undesirable behavior, and the object may not rotate as intended.

To avoid gimbal lock and achieve smooth and expected rotations, it is usually recommended to use a quaternion-based approach for rotations or a matrix-based transformation instead of applying rotations individually on separate nodes. These approaches can offer more precise and predictable rotations in 3D space.
so how do i mimic and apply that 1 degree rotation on each axis nodes that has the same behavior as 1 node that i mentioned earier, regardless of its current orientation.

To mimic the behavior of applying a 1 degree rotation on each axis on separate nodes while maintaining the same behavior as a single node, you can use a hierarchical structure where the nodes are organized in a parent-child relationship. 

Here's an example of how you can achieve this:

1. Create a parent node (let's call it "parentNode").
2. Create three child nodes under the parent node, one for each axis of rotation (let's call them "xNode", "yNode", and "zNode").
3. Attach the entity to the "zNode".
4. Apply the rotation in sequence: first, rotate the "xNode" by 1 degree around the X-axis, then rotate the "yNode" by 1 degree around the Y-axis, and finally, rotate the "zNode" by 1 degree around the Z-axis.
5. Update the rotation every frame to maintain the continuous rotation effect.

By organizing the nodes in this hierarchical structure and applying the rotations individually on each child node, you can achieve the same rotational behavior as applying a 1 degree rotation on a single node regardless of its current orientation.
'void Ogre::Node::rotate(const Ogre::Quaternion &,Ogre::Node::TransformSpace)' : cannot convert from 'Ogre::Radian (__cdecl *)(Ogre::Degree)' to 'const Ogre::Vector3 &'
what happened to the TS_PARENT and use rotate instead that you were talking about earlier?

it's wobbling instead of spinning on all 3 axis when 1 degree across is applied per frame
its your code that is at fault. i did present you a better solution earlier, only it does not work on a specific starting angle.
